author: @dt-tv4fs
text: @ 14:26 you say "SCR" but later say SSR and your diagram shows SSR. Thanks for your efforts, your audio has 3 problems: 1 voice pitch changes, echo from walls, speaking style (emphasis) that intersect and even tho i wear headphones i find a few sounds unavailable to translation. However MOST of the time you repeat certain words and if i wait I can usually get the acronym (like PET) at a later time. Thanks again, this video helped me think i can build an electric kiln.!

author: @RWBHere
text:
That looks like a solid state relay. It will not be a rectifier. That aside, your information is sound. 
No pun intended there. The sound is the trademark Robbietone™ which we're accustomed to having on this fascinating channel.

Cheers, 
Sorin

author: @KingNast
text: There are a ton of counterfeit Fotek ssr's in eBay and Amazon. It's hard to find a real one.  The fake ones are really inexpensive and have no amp rating. They also usually say "made in Taiwan" instead of "Taiwan made". I wouldn't trust those for anything but intermittent use with low amperage

replies.author: @garbleduser
replies.text: This has been a huge problem lately. This is why you need to use the alarm relay as an emergency cutoff!

replies.author: @KingNast
replies.text: @garbleduser  That's a good idea.  I got an Opto 22 ssr made in USA and it seems pretty decent. It hasn't seen much use though. I think it was around $22 USD

replies.author: @garbleduser
replies.text: I just open the poorly potted SCRs and replace the switching component, and the suspect supporting components. I have found a trend in both the optocouplers and low value resistors being the primary cause of failure.

author: @William_Hada
text: Great explanation for using these valuable controllers Rob!  PID controllers are so versatile and cheap. I use them for controlling cheap kitchen toaster ovens and can control the temperature within + or - 1 degree centigrade.

replies.author: @mihaiaragon
replies.text:
Hi Will, do you know how to control the rate of centigrade/min from this type of PID controller?
Cheers,
Sorin

replies.author: @William_Hada
replies.text: @mihaiaragon Hello Sorin! Yes, I know how to control the rate of temperature, which is called the " ramp ". However you need to have a specialized PID that is able to do ramp/soak. These specialized PID's cost more than the common type of PID. They cost about $80USD on eBay vs $25USD for the common type that can't do a ramp.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely agree mate - i use them all the time - they are quick easy and reliable with pretty good accuracy

text: nice 👍 Thank you 🙏

replies.author: @AlienRelics
replies.text:
Sadly, he has repeatedly stated that he will not use a lapel or shotgun mike, going so far as to say that he tosses all the microphones people have mailed to him.


Also sadly, I've stopped watching for the most part because I just can't decipher everything he is saying.

replies.author: @nophead
replies.text:
@AlienRelics Bad mike placement will make it faint and echoey but there also seems to some digital compression that makes it sound like it is underwater. The sort of effect you get with live video feeds when the bandwidth is too low. I wonder if he just uploads it live.


Love the content but struggle to hear it.

replies.author: @AlienRelics
replies.text: @nophead I love all of his content, but I just can't. He's using the mic on the camera. At least, the last time he spoke about it.

author: @ancapftw9113
text:
I'm thinking of making a CO2 scrubber out of limestone.  This could help me get to the proper (865 C) temperature to bake the CO2 out of the limestone.


Now I just need to work out the valves to route where the CO2 exhaust goes and where the intake air comes from.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Bubble air through an algae-filled tank for air scrubbing but for some reason, I think you might be building for a submarine or similar project so maybe not.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text:
@Barskor1 that works for some cases, but I want a machine that I can use to add/remove co2 from a room on demand.


At first I would use it to regulate CO2 in a greenhouse.  Eventually, I want to build a self contained living area in a cave or mine, to simulate a Mars base.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text:
@ancapftw9113 Interesting so the carbon levels in the greenhouse are projected to be very high possibly life endangering for fast plant growth so when you want to go in and tend the place that has to drop very fast?
The base idea sounds amazing I wish I could do that.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: @Barskor1 pretty much.  another experiment I want to run is to see how little oxygen plants can survive on.  That way on planets with a lot of CO2 but no oxygen you can have the plants do most of the air processing for you instead of building a machine to do it.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @ancapftw9113 That is an excellent experiment and plan.

author: @scottwojo7881
text: I am an inventor in Ohio.   I love this feed.   You have led me to believe that I could make a space heater from graphene.  Do you have suggestions for laying out the material?  Do you layout in a pattern similar to a toaster element or in a complete thin layer across an area with the wire at opposite ends?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: He has a video on making space heaters with his graphene ink IRC those questions are answered in it. Hope that helps.

author: @AussieIan100
text: Does the Solid State Rectifier turn AC to DC in this case?  Wouldn't think that would be necessary.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: with it needing an input voltage, I think it's a solid state relay.

author: @AussieIan100
text: I always knew them as "Solid State Relays" but I guess in Britain they call them "Solid State Rectifiers".  Ian.  Aussie Enjoying Oregon.  By the way I have really enjoyed all of your videos!!

replies.author: @AlienRelics
replies.text: He mis-spoke, it is "solid state relay", not "solid state rectifier".
